b' Money Stuff
Actually this is a greenwashing case: US prosecutors charged Gautam Adani with helping drive a $250 million bribery scheme, in a stunning bl

Adani

Actually this is a greenwashing case :

US prosecutors charged Gautam Adani with helping drive a $250 million bribery scheme, in a stunning blow to India\xe2\x80\x99s most powerful businessman and close ally of Prime Minister Narendra Modi.

Federal prosecutors alleged on Wednesday that Adani, one of the world\xe2\x80\x99s richest people, and other defendants promised to pay more than $250 million in bribes to Indian government officials to win solar energy contracts, and concealed the plan as they sought to raise money from US investors. US law allows federal prosecutors to pursue foreign corruption allegations if they involve certain links to American investors or markets.

Here are the press releases from the US Department of Justice and the Securities and Exchange Commission , charging Adani and several alleged co-conspirators with violating the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (the anti-bribery law) and also committing securities fraud.

It\xe2\x80\x99s a little weird, no?\xc2\xa0Indian executives of an Indian company listed in India who allegedly paid bribes to Indian government officials to win Indian government contracts were charged with crimes in the US . Paying bribes to government officials seems like it must be a crime, but why is it a crime in the US to pay those bribes in India ?

The gist of the allegations (from the SEC complaint ) is that Adani Green Energy Ltd., an Indian public company that is part of the Adani Group, [1] was in the business of developing solar and wind power projects in India. It won \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0apparently legitimately \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0a big contract from the Indian federal government\xc2\xa0to build solar plants and sell power, a contract worth \xe2\x80\x9cbillions of dollars of revenue and more than a billion dollars in profit.\xe2\x80\x9d But actually the award didn\xe2\x80\x99t commit the government to buying power from Adani Green. First, the government would need to enter into power purchasing contracts with Indian state government power companies, and the state governments refused to pay the prices that the federal government had agreed to. (\xe2\x80\x9cThe price for energy capacity that [the federal government agency] had tentatively agreed to pay under the Letters of Award turned out to be too high,\xe2\x80\x9d in part \xe2\x80\x9cbecause aspects of the Indian renewable energy market had shifted and caused downward pressure on solar energy prices.\xe2\x80\x9d) So, says the SEC:

Executives of both Adani Green and Azure [Global Power Ltd., its partner on the deal, formerly listed in the US], including [Gautam Adani\xe2\x80\x99s nephew] Sagar Adani, began to pressure and to propose to pay \xe2\x80\x9cincentives\xe2\x80\x9d (i.e., bribes) directly to Indian state government officials to persuade them to cause the Indian state governments or the state-owned DISCOMs [energy distribution companies] to agree to Power Supply Agreements with SECI [the federal agency] at prices favorable to Adani and Azure.

I have to say, we talk from time to time around here about good and bad ways to describe bribery , and this is the good way. (I mean, to describe alleged bribery.) If you told me \xe2\x80\x9cwe won a contract with an intermediary to supply power at a fixed rate, but the ultimate buyers balked because that rate is above-market, so we gave those buyers cash incentives to get them to agree to the contract,\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0I\xe2\x80\x99d be like \xe2\x80\x9cthat\xe2\x80\x99s a little weird but I\xe2\x80\x99m sure there\xe2\x80\x99s some reason you want to sell at the contract rate, so paying incentives to make the ultimate buyers economically indifferent is probably fine.\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0And then if you said \xe2\x80\x9cwell, technically the state governments are paying the contract rate, while the cash incentives are being paid to the state government officials in their personal accounts,\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0I\xe2\x80\x99d be like \xe2\x80\x9cah\xc2\xa0you got me you rascal, those are bribes!\xe2\x80\x9d

But having a line item in the budget for \xe2\x80\x9cincentives\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0strikes me as much better than having one for \xe2\x80\x9cbribes,\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0or for \xe2\x80\x9cnudge nudge,\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0or maybe even for \xe2\x80\x9cconsulting fees.\xe2\x80\x9d The SEC complaints and the criminal indictment\xc2\xa0do not present an overwhelming case that the \xe2\x80\x9cincentives\xe2\x80\x9d were paid to the government officials personally, but:

Gautam Adani, with Sagar Adani\xe2\x80\x99s assistance, ultimately paid or promised bribes to government officials in numerous Indian states worth hundreds of millions of dollars to cause those state governments and their officials to enter into Power Supply Agreements with SECI. Adani Green\xe2\x80\x99s internal records documented these payments or promises.

Again, having your internal records document the incentives is good! Makes them look like incentives, not bags of cash delivered under the table. Or the criminal indictment lists some allegedly incriminating communications, all of which could plausibly be legitimate:

On or about November 24, 2020, [Azure CEO Ranjit] GUPTA exchanged electronic messages with the defendant SAGAR R. ADANI regarding efforts to convince states to purchase power under the Manufacturing Linked Project, as part of which GUPTA wrote, \xe2\x80\x9cthe advantage we have is that the discoms are being motivated.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cThe state power companies are being motivated\xe2\x80\x9d is good! What you don\xe2\x80\x99t want is \xe2\x80\x9cthe state power company executives are being motivated,\xe2\x80\x9d but he didn\xe2\x80\x99t say that!\xc2\xa0This is obviously not legal advice and didn\xe2\x80\x99t work, but still.

Also from the criminal indictment:

When communicating about the bribery scheme, [various alleged conspirators] often referred to the defendants GAUTAM S. ADANI and [Adani Green Chief Executive Officer] VNEET S. JAAIN by code names. Specifically, among other things, [they] referred to GAUTAM S. ADANI as \xe2\x80\x9cSAG,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cMr[.] A,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cNumero uno\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cthe big man,\xe2\x80\x9d and referred to JAAIN as \xe2\x80\x9cV,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9csnake\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cNumero uno minus one.\xe2\x80\x9d

Okay those are not code names, those are at most nicknames . (\xe2\x80\x9cMr. A,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cSAG\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cV\xe2\x80\x9d aren\xe2\x80\x99t even nicknames, they\xe2\x80\x99re just initials, come on.) I promise that, in the last hour, hundreds of employees of dozens of companies have sent entirely legitimate and routine business emails referring to their bosses not by name but as \xe2\x80\x9cthe big man,\xe2\x80\x9d and there have probably been a few perfectly legitimate \xe2\x80\x9csnakes\xe2\x80\x9d too. [2] \xe2\x80\x9cNumero uno minus one\xe2\x80\x9d is very funny however.

Anyway! Why is this a US crime? Part of the answer is that Azure was listed in the US until 2023. But another important part of the answer is that Adani Green sold $750 million of dollar-denominated bonds in 2021, about $175 million of which went to US investors. And those investors, says the SEC, were misled:

In connection with its offer and sale of the Notes and before it sold any Notes, Adani Green provided the Offering Circulars to potential investors. \xe2\x80\xa6

The Offering Circulars also repeatedly disclosed to investors that an \xe2\x80\x9cintegral\xe2\x80\x9d part of Adani Green\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cphilosophy\xe2\x80\x9d is its \xe2\x80\x9cenvironmental, social, governance (\xe2\x80\x98ESG\xe2\x80\x99) policy\xe2\x80\x9d and that Adani Green operates pursuant to an \xe2\x80\x9cESG Framework.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\xa6

The Offering Circulars highlighted to potential investors that, as part of Adani Green\xe2\x80\x99s commitment to ESG principles, it is a \xe2\x80\x9cparticipant of the United Nations Global Compact, committing [Adani Green] to supporting the ten principles of the United Nations Global Compact in human rights, labor, environment and anti-corruption.\xe2\x80\x9d Principle 10 of the United Nations Global Compact, signed by Adani Green and highlighted in connection with the Offering and the Notes, says that \xe2\x80\x9cBusinesses should work against corruption in all its forms, including extortion and bribery.\xe2\x80\x9d

The Offering Circulars then disclosed to potential investors that a \xe2\x80\x9ccore\xe2\x80\x9d part of the success of Adani Group \xe2\x80\x94 of which Adani Green is a part \xe2\x80\x94 is its philosophy of \xe2\x80\x9cGrowth with Goodness\xe2\x80\x9d and its commitment to ESG principles. \xe2\x80\xa6

The Offering Circulars then conveyed that Adani Green\xe2\x80\x99s efforts related to environmental, social responsibility, and good corporate governance principles \xe2\x80\x94 which included Adani Green\xe2\x80\x99s purported efforts with respect to anti-bribery and anti-corruption \xe2\x80\x94 should be meaningful to investors\xe2\x80\x99 investment decisions. \xe2\x80\xa6

The Offering Circulars\xe2\x80\x99 many statements representing to potential investors that a core tenet of Adani Green and its Board was preventing bribery and corruption gave any reasonable investor comfort that none of Adani Green\xe2\x80\x99s executives or directors were then involved in a corrupt bribery scheme. This was misleading. In fact, months and weeks earlier, Adani Green\xe2\x80\x99s leadership, Gautam Adani and Sagar Adani, had been personally involved in such a corrupt bribery scheme, a fact that was not disclosed in the Offering Circulars.

Those bonds were repaid in full earlier this year, so the US investors who bought them didn\xe2\x80\x99t lose any money. Arguably they were still defrauded, because they bought bonds that were financially riskier than they expected: The standard argument is \xe2\x80\x9cthe bribes make Adani\xe2\x80\x99s business riskier, and if the market knew about them, the bonds would have priced at a higher yield.\xe2\x80\x9d (And in fact Adani Group bonds sold off when the charges were announced. [3] ) But that is a little unsatisfying: Adani Green sold the bonds to investors and then paid them back as promised, so while they were arguably defrauded in expectation, they did not actually lose any money.

But that\xe2\x80\x99s not the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s argument. Rather, the argument is that the investors were defrauded because they thought the bonds were ESG , but the bonds were not in fact ESG. Or, rather, the bonds were issued to fund solar projects, yes, but their social and governance credentials were suspect, what with the bribes:

In its annual reports, news releases, and other self-published documents, Adani Green has positioned itself as a leader in environmentally conscious, socially responsible, and good corporate governance principles, often referred to as environmental, social, and governance or \xe2\x80\x9cESG\xe2\x80\x9d principles. In this way, Adani Green has sought to differentiate itself from its peers and other potential investments or issuers in developing countries that might be susceptible to corruption and bribery issues and to specifically appeal to investors who prioritize ESG principles or ESG-related investments.

Back in 2021, this was a real thing. Investors wanted to be ESG: They wanted to put their money into companies that had good environmental, social and governance performance. A large, stable, revenue-generating company that raised a lot of money in international capital markets and built solar power plants in developing countries was extremely attractive to a certain class of international ESG-focused investors, but it would be significantly less attractive if it disclosed \xe2\x80\x9calso we pay tons of bribes.\xe2\x80\x9d Pretending to be very ESG, while in fact (allegedly) being a solar power company that paid\xc2\xa0bribes, really was a way to defraud investors. But the fraud was less \xe2\x80\x9cyou took their money and didn\xe2\x80\x99t give it back\xe2\x80\x9d and more \xe2\x80\x9cyou took their money and didn\xe2\x80\x99t give them what they really wanted, which was good ESG performance.\xe2\x80\x9d That is the crime here.

Not for long, though, maybe. I wrote last week that \xe2\x80\x9cGary Gensler\xe2\x80\x99s SEC has a few months to bring its last greenwashing cases,\xe2\x80\x9d but that the Trump administration will presumably be anti-ESG and will sue companies for doing ESG stuff, not for falsely pretending to do ESG stuff. Also \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0I mean \xe2\x80\xa6 bribes \xe2\x80\xa6 you know \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0never mind. \xe2\x80\x9cProsecuting the case would take months, if not years, meaning that it will fall to the incoming Trump administration\xe2\x80\x99s Justice Department to determine how to proceed,\xe2\x80\x9d notes Bloomberg News . But for now, it\xe2\x80\x99s illegal in the US for Indian companies to pay bribes in India.

Rated feeders

Banks and insurance companies have risk-based capital requirements. You need to have more capital against risky assets than against safe assets. Schematically, it might go something like:

  1. You need 2% capital against AAA-rated bonds.
  2. You need 6% capital against BB-rated bonds.
  3. You need 8% capital against unrated bonds.
  4. You need 30% capital against equity exposures.

Something like that, though these are not real numbers. So if you buy $100 of unrated bonds, you need $8 of capital, because those bonds are risky.

Are they? An essential insight of finance is that they are partially risky. One hundred dollars of unrated bonds might not pay back the full $100, but they\xe2\x80\x99ll probably pay back at least, you know, $60, even if things go really wrong. So what you do is you put the bonds in a box and slice the cash flows into new bonds:

  1. The first $60 of payments go to the Tranche A bonds, which get an AAA rating.
  2. The next $30 of payments go to the Tranche B bonds, which get a BB rating.
  3. The last $10 of payments to got the equity tranche, which doesn\xe2\x80\x99t get a rating.

And then you buy all of the tranches. Now you need $1.20 of capital (2% of $60) against the Tranche A bonds, $1.80 of capital (6% of $30) against the Tranche B bonds, and $3 of capital (30% of $10) against the equity tranche.\xc2\xa0That\xe2\x80\x99s a total of $6 of capital, which is less than the $8 you\xe2\x80\x99d need against the unsliced bonds. Again, these numbers are not real, they\xe2\x80\x99re just to give you a sense of the possibilities.

The point is that there are two things going on here. One is a real thing: If you slice a pot of cash flows into junior and senior claims, the senior claims really are safer than the unsliced pot, so they should get a better rating, lower capital requirements, etc.

But the other is a pure regulatory arbitrage: If you slice a pot of cash flows into junior and senior claims, and then buy all of them , the total of all of the claims can\xe2\x80\x99t be safer than the unsliced pot. But the capital requirements are always going to be a bit arbitrary, and maybe you can find a slicing that gets you a better result than leaving the pot alone.

We have talked about this before , but here\xe2\x80\x99s a good Bloomberg News story on rated feeders :

The vehicles, known as rated feeders, package stakes in private debt funds into bonds, making it cheaper for insurers to buy them by effectively cutting the amount of capital that they need to hold against those investments. \xe2\x80\xa6

Rated feeders are part of a broader push by private credit firms to convince the US insurance industry, which controls trillions in long-term capital, to pour money into the asset class. In fact, for many of private lending\xe2\x80\x99s biggest players, insurers have become so critical to their growth efforts that they\xe2\x80\x99ve built out or bought large insurance units of their own.

Rated feeders allow those units and other insurers to funnel cash into private credit \xe2\x80\x94 which typically finances highly leveraged companies and offers juicier yields relative to conventional bonds and loans \xe2\x80\x94 in a capital efficient way, backers argue. ...

A private credit firm looking to raise capital will create a standalone investment vehicle to house a stake in its fund. Insurance investors will often buy a vertical slice of the securitization in the form of high-grade and junk-rated bonds as well as a subordinated equity tranche, in exchange receiving a share of the interest and principal payments from the assets in the fund.

Buying the vehicles lets insurers sharply reduce the money they must set aside to back their exposure to the private credit fund, since US insurance industry rules apply capital charges of 30% or more to holdings of fund shares, compared to low-to-mid single digits for most debt.

\xe2\x80\x9cFund shares\xe2\x80\x9d are, categorically, risky, so they get a 30% capital charge. \xe2\x80\x9cInvestment-grade bonds\xe2\x80\x9d are, categorically, safe, so they get a 2% capital charge. Most of the fund shares can be repackaged into investment-grade bonds. So they are.

AllHere

If you run a private artificial intelligence startup, how much are you allowed to lie to investors? The answer is not \xe2\x80\x9cnone,\xe2\x80\x9d surely. This is not legal advice. Legally , the rules for private startups are roughly the same as the rules for public stock offerings: You\xe2\x80\x99re not allowed to lie to investors about material facts to get money out of them. But practically , venture capital investors are looking for visionary founders and are willing to accept a certain amount of fraud. If you call some of your one-off revenue \xe2\x80\x9cannual recurring revenue\xe2\x80\x9d to make your numbers look better, your VCs might not catch you, and if they do they might not complain, and if they do complain, they might complain privately to you and not embarrass themselves by going to the police.

I tend to think \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and this is very much not legal advice \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0that to get arrested for startup fraud, you often have to\xc2\xa0(1) do a certain amount of fudging of the numbers plus (2) do some egregious embarrassing additional stunt. We have talked a few times about Carlos Watson , the founder of Ozy Media, who was criminally charged and convicted for defrauding his startup\xe2\x80\x99s investors. Watson\xe2\x80\x99s defense was, sure, he made exaggerated claims about the company\xe2\x80\x99s business, but every startup founder\xc2\xa0does that, that\xe2\x80\x99s not a crime. That defense is not legally viable, and didn\xe2\x80\x99t work with a jury, but you can sort of see why it would be a little sympathetic. The problem is that \xe2\x80\x9cWatson coached his co-founder to impersonate a YouTube executive [on a due diligence call] to get Goldman Sachs bankers to back the media startup,\xe2\x80\x9d and that is crazy. If you do that , your investors will call the police.

Anyway this isn\xe2\x80\x99t quite that but it\xe2\x80\x99s close :

The founder of an artificial intelligence start-up focused on education was arrested and charged with defrauding her investors, lying about the company\xe2\x80\x99s profits and falsely claiming that some of the largest school districts in the country, including New York City\xe2\x80\x99s, were her customers.

The founder, Joanna Smith-Griffin, started the company, AllHere Education, in 2016, with the goal of using artificial intelligence to increase student and parent engagement and curb absenteeism.

In the years that followed, Ms. Smith-Griffin, 33, misrepresented AllHere\xe2\x80\x99s revenue and customer base to fraudulently raise almost $10 million in funds, according to the indictment. Once the company\xe2\x80\x99s valuation had climbed, she sold some of her stake in it and spent hundreds of thousands of dollars on a down payment for a new home and on her wedding.

Here are the Justice Department announcement and indictment , and some of it is garden-variety stuff. (Prosecutors complain that, in her Series A Investor Deck,\xc2\xa0there was a page about customers that includes the logos of several school districts that were not actually AllHere customers, though with no explicit claim that they were.)

But the collapse of the fraud is a bit much: In May 2024, Smith-Griffin was late sending out financial statements to her investors, so a VC investor\xc2\xa0contacted AllHere\xe2\x80\x99s outside accounting firm directly, and the accounting firm \xe2\x80\x9csent Investor-1 AllHere\xe2\x80\x99s true Q1 2024 financials,\xe2\x80\x9d which were a lot worse than the fake ones Smith-Griffin had allegedly\xc2\xa0been sending. The investor got suspicious, the board of directors started asking questions, and \xe2\x80\x9cas part of that reconciliation, on or about June 10, 2024, Consultant-1, an outside financial consultant to AllHere, participated in a video teleconference call with Director-1 and Director-2,\xe2\x80\x9d two outside directors representing AllHere\xe2\x80\x99s VC investors.

During that call, Smith-Griffin allegedly emailed the directors from a fake email account under the consultant\xe2\x80\x99s name. During the call! What do you think happened?

Director-1 told Consultant-1 during their video call that she had received Consultant-1\xe2\x80\x99s email. Consultant-1 responded that Consultant-1 did not have an AllHere email account, did not send that email, and had not been in contact with JOANNA SMITH GRIFFIN, the defendant, since approximately in or about April 2024, over a month prior.

The board more or less immediately fired her and, one assumes, went to the police. You can\xe2\x80\x99t impersonate people on calls with investors! Why does this keep coming up?

Crypto winter is over

Bitcoin is nearing but this is probably the most bullish crypto news I\xe2\x80\x99ve seen in two years :

\xe2\x80\x9cComedian,\xe2\x80\x9d by Italian artist Maurizio Cattelan, sold for $6.2 million at auction Wednesday night.

The piece consists of an ordinary, yellow banana affixed to a white wall with a diagonal piece of silver duct tape. Since Cattelan debuted the work at Art Basel Miami Beach five years ago, what\xe2\x80\x99s become known as the Banana has turned into a viral sensation. It\xe2\x80\x99s attracted crowds, copycats, and even its own cryptocurrency. \xe2\x80\xa6

Ultimately it sold to Chinese collector Justin Sun, founder of cryptocurrency platform Tron. He intends to pay for the piece in crypto. After the sale, Sun said he considered the piece to be historic, but he also said he has plans of his own for the Banana: \xe2\x80\x9cIn the coming days, I will personally eat the banana as part of this unique artistic experience, honoring its place in both art history and popular culture.\xe2\x80\x9d

A year ago, the guy who bought and ate the banana would have been Ken Griffin. Now it\xe2\x80\x99s extremely crypto guy Justin Sun. There is an obvious affinity between a crypto boom\xc2\xa0and contemporary art collecting, in that:

  • a crypto boom makes a lot of people fantastically wealthy quickly and without much effort, so they have money to spend; and
  • it feels like a joke, so they might be inclined to spend their money on jokes, and the contemporary art market produces a lot of expensive jokes.

And so in the last crypto boom lots of art, particularly this sort of art, was bought by crypto entrepreneurs. And now we are back.\xc2\xa0For the record:

  1. \xe2\x80\x9cComedian\xe2\x80\x9d is obviously good and funny.
  2. I hate to hand it to Sun, but\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x9cI will personally eat the banana\xe2\x80\x9d is also obviously good and funny.
  3. There has been some crypto drama about a \xe2\x80\x9cComedian\xe2\x80\x9d-inspired memecoin, $BAN,\xc2\xa0apparently launched by a Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s employee on \xe2\x80\x9cthe Pump.Fun platform\xe2\x80\x9d; I cannot personally bring myself to care about it but perhaps you disagree.

Pivot to AI

Though, counterpoint :

Barry Silbert, the founder of the beleaguered crypto conglomerate Digital Currency Group, unveiled plans Wednesday to launch a decentralized artificial-intelligence company called Yuma.

The company will provide capital for entrepreneurs to build applications on the Bittensor network, a fledgling blockchain that is still largely unknown outside crypto circles. ...

\xe2\x80\x9cThere is a lot of hype because there is a lot of potential,\xe2\x80\x9d said Noelle Acheson, a crypto macro strategist. \xe2\x80\x9cI am skeptical because I just don\xe2\x80\x99t think the AI users are going to care that much\xe2\x80\x9d about whether the network is decentralized.

\xe2\x80\x9cThe question always is: Can you do this without a blockchain?\xe2\x80\x9d she said.

Some of the intellectual work of crypto firms and entrepreneurs pivoting to AI involves saying \xe2\x80\x9cactually, in a sense, crypto really is AI, so really we were right all along.\xe2\x80\x9d Thus, AI, but on the blockchain.

Things happen

Thrive Capital : the venture firm staking billions on a few big bets. Elon Musk\xe2\x80\x99s xAI Startup Is Valued at $50 Billion in New Funding Round. BofA targets regional banks to expand risk transfer market . Forest-Preservation Credits Win Seal of Approval From Industry Watchdog. UK Households Will Get Paid to Not Use Power at Peak Times. Hedge Fund Two Sigma Cuts 200 Staff After Strategic Review. The US Stablecoin Startup Fueling a $3 Billion Boom in Africa. Bill Hwang Sentenced to 18 Years in Prison in Archegos Case.\xc2\xa0The Lender Bailing Out Desperate Luxury Property Owners \xe2\x80\x94for a Price.\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x98Wicked\xe2\x80\x99 Fans Singing in Movie Theaters Are Anything but Popular. Crude oil baths .

If you'd like to get\xc2\xa0Money\xc2\xa0Stuff\xc2\xa0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please subscribe at this link . Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here . Thanks!

[1] From the SEC complaint : \xe2\x80\x9cAdani Group (or the \xe2\x80\x98Group\xe2\x80\x99) is a privately held multinational conglomerate headquartered in Ahmedabad, India, with numerous entities throughout India and in, among other places, Australia, Indonesia, Mauritius, Panama, Singapore, and the United Arab Emirates. Gautam Adani formed Adani Group in 1988 as a commodity trading firm and subsequently expanded it to own and operate airports, shipping ports, and railways; to produce and distribute power and energy through mining and thermal and renewable energy production; and to be India\xe2\x80\x99s largest trader of coal. Adani Group\xe2\x80\x99s holdings currently have a market capitalization of more than $200 billion. Adani Green (or the \xe2\x80\x98Issuer\xe2\x80\x99) is a public limited company formed by Gautam Adani and Rajesh Adani in 2015 under the laws of India, with a principal place of business in Ahmedabad, to be the renewable energy arm of Adani Group.\xe2\x80\x9d

[2] When I was an investment banker my desk sometimes referred to a colleague \xe2\x80\x94 affectionately! \xe2\x80\x94 as \xe2\x80\x9cHead of the Snake,\xe2\x80\x9d but we were weird.

[3] The criminal indictment notes that Adani Green issued new bonds in 2024, and charges that those bonds too involved fraud (because of the past bribes).
